title: Connect a Local Git Repository to a Remote on GitHub and Push Your Local Branch

steps.title: Verify the Local Git Repository
steps.instruction: Type git status in the terminal and press enter. Verify that there are some commits already in the log and you are on the main branch.

steps.title: Create a New Empty Repository on GitHub
steps.instruction: Open your browser and go to github.com. Create a new empty repository by clicking the plus icon and selecting new repository.

steps.title: Configure the New Repository
steps.instruction: Give it a name. Do not check the initialize this repository with a readme option.

steps.title: Find the Push Commands
steps.instruction: GitHub will now show you a page with setup instructions since the repository is empty. Look for the section that says push an existing repository from the command line.

steps.title: Copy the First Command
steps.instruction: Copy the first command: git remote add origin followed by the https or ssh url of your repository.

steps.title: Add the Remote Repository
steps.instruction: Switch back to your terminal. Paste and run the git remote add origin command. Press Enter.

steps.title: Verify the Remote Repository
steps.instruction: Type git remote -v and press Enter. Verify that origin is listed twice, once for fetch and once for push, both pointing to your GitHub repository URL.

steps.title: Push the Local Branch
steps.instruction: Type git push -u origin main and press enter. The -u flag sets the upstream tracking relationship.

steps.title: Authenticate with GitHub
steps.instruction: You may be prompted to enter your GitHub username and a personal access token as the password. If you haven't set up a personal access token yet, you'll need to create one in GitHub under Settings Developer Settings Personal Access Tokens.

steps.title: Verify the Push
steps.instruction: Once authentication succeeds, Git will upload your commits and you should see output showing the objects being written and a confirmation that your branch was pushed.

steps.title: Verify the Repository on GitHub
steps.instruction: Switch back to your browser and refresh the GitHub repository page. Verify that all your files and commit history are now visible.
